---
title: 'FingerHut Project'
subtitle: 'Sampling and Flattening process'
author: 'Charlie Kopp'
date: today
date-format: long
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false

---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(data.table)
library(collapse)
library(arrow)
dir.create("data/processed", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
```

# Read Data

```{r}
#| label: read data

dt <- setDT(read_parquet("data/processed/dt_clean2.parquet"))
user_outcomes <- setDT(read_parquet("data/processed/user_outcomes2.parquet"))
```

# Separate Complete and Incomplete (test and train) Users

```{r}
#| label: separate complete and incomplete users

test_ids <- user_outcomes[final_outcome == "incomplete", id]
train_ids <- user_outcomes[final_outcome != "incomplete", id]
```

# Define flattening function

```{r}
#| label: flatten function
flatten_journey <- function(events_dt) {
	setorder(events_dt, id, event_timestamp)

	# OPTIMIZATION: Create temporary integer columns for fast math.
	# This leaves original event_timestamp completely untouched for later use
	events_dt[, ts_int := as.integer(event_timestamp)]
	events_dt[, cutoff_int := as.integer(cutoff_time)]

	# OPTIMIZATION: Vectorize the gaps globally.
	# Calculating shift() inside a grouped block is slow. Doing it here is near-instant.
	events_dt[, gap_s := ts_int - shift(ts_int), by = id]

	# 1. Grouped Feature Engineering
	summary_dt <- events_dt[,
		{
			first_ts <- event_timestamp[1]
			last_ts <- event_timestamp[.N]

			first_ts_int <- ts_int[1]
			last_ts_int <- ts_int[.N]
			user_cutoff_int <- cutoff_int[1]

			.(
				first_action_ts = first_ts,
				last_action_ts = last_ts,

				# MATH HACK 1: Seconds length is just max minus min
				journey_length_s = last_ts_int - first_ts_int,

				# MATH HACK 2: Days is just the seconds difference divided by 86,400
				days_inactive = as.integer((user_cutoff_int - last_ts_int) / 86400),

				total_actions = .N,

				mean_gap_sec = if (.N > 1) mean(gap_s, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
				median_gap_sec = if (.N > 1) fmedian(gap_s, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
				max_gap_sec = if (.N > 1) max(gap_s, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_integer_
			)
		},
		by = id
	]

  # CLEANUP: Remove the temporary math columns
  events_dt[, c("ts_int", "cutoff_int", "gap_s") := NULL]

	# 2. Wide Event Counts
	counts <- dcast(
		events_dt,
		id ~ event_name,
		value.var = "event_name",
		fun.aggregate = length,
		fill = 0
	)
	setnames(
		counts,
		setdiff(names(counts), "id"),
		paste0("n_", setdiff(names(counts), "id"))
	)

	# 3. Merge and return
	return(merge(summary_dt, counts, by = "id"))
}
```

# Flatten test set

```{r}
#| label: flatten test set

test_events <- dt[id %in% test_ids]
test_events[, cutoff_time := max(event_timestamp)]
test_flat <- flatten_journey(test_events)
test_flat[, final_outcome := "incomplete"]

write_parquet(test_flat, "data/processed/test_features.parquet")

rm(test_events, test_flat); gc()
```

# Sample cut-points from train set

## 1. Simple Sample

- single random cutpoint from each complete journey

```{r}
#| label: simple single sample
# 1. Filter raw events for Train IDs
train_events <- dt[id %in% train_ids]

# 2. Generate exactly one random time cutoff per user
set.seed(42)
cutoffs <- train_events[,
  .(
    start_ts = min(event_timestamp),
    end_ts = max(event_timestamp)
  ),
  by = id
]

# Generate a random timestamp between start and end
cutoffs[,
  random_cutoff := start_ts +
    runif(.N) * as.numeric(difftime(end_ts, start_ts, units = "secs"))
]

# 3. Join the cutoffs back to the events and filter

train_events <- train_events[cutoffs[, .(id, random_cutoff)], on = "id"]
train_events_truncated <- train_events[event_timestamp <= random_cutoff]
setnames(train_events_truncated, "random_cutoff", "cutoff_time")

# 4. Flatten the truncated training data
# Notice we pass 'random_cutoff' so days_inactive is calculated relative to the snapshot time
train_flat <- flatten_journey(train_events_truncated)

# 5. Bring in the true labels from step 1
# We must use the TRUE final outcome we calculated earlier, not the truncated outcome!

train_flat <- merge(train_flat, user_outcomes[id %in% train_ids], by = "id")

saveRDS(train_flat, "data/processed/train_features_simple_sample.rds")

rm(train_events, train_events_truncated, train_flat, cutoffs); gc()

# train_feats <- readRDS("data/processed/train_features_simple_sample.rds")

# hist((train_feats$journey_length_s))

# rm(train_feats); gc()
```


## 2. Sample proportional to total_actions


```{r}
#| label: generate K cutoffs

train_events <- dt[id %in% train_ids]

set.seed(42)

# 1. Summarize user journeys to determine K
snapshot_counts <- train_events[, .(
  total_actions = .N,
  start_ts      = event_timestamp[1],
  end_ts        = event_timestamp[.N]
), by = id]

snapshot_counts <- merge(snapshot_counts, user_outcomes[, .(id, final_outcome)], by = "id")

# 2. Define K: bounded between 1 and 5

# frank(..., ties.method = "min") safely handles the heavy skew
# It ranks users 1 to N, divides by N to get a percentile (0 to 1), multiplies by 5, and rounds up.
snapshot_counts[, K := ceiling(5 * (frank(total_actions, ties.method = "min") / .N))]

# 3. Expand the table: Duplicate rows so each user has K rows
cutoffs <- snapshot_counts[rep(1:.N, K)]

# 4. Generate random cutoffs and a unique snapshot_id
cutoffs[, random_cutoff := start_ts + runif(.N) * as.numeric(difftime(end_ts, start_ts, units="secs"))]

# Create a unique ID for each snapshot (e.g., "User123_1", "User123_2")
cutoffs[, snapshot_id := paste0(id, "_", rowid(id))]
```

## 3. Sample proportional to journey_days


```{r}
#| label: generate K cutoffs per user based on journey_days

train_events <- dt[id %in% train_ids]

set.seed(13)

# 1. Summarize user journeys to determine K
snapshot_counts <- train_events[, .(
  total_actions = .N,
  start_ts      = event_timestamp[1],
  end_ts        = event_timestamp[.N]
), by = id][
  , journey_days := as.numeric(
    difftime(end_ts, start_ts, units="days"))]

snapshot_counts <- merge(snapshot_counts, user_outcomes[, .(id, final_outcome)], by = "id")

# 2. Define K: bounded between 1 and 5

# frank(..., ties.method = "min") safely handles the heavy skew
# It ranks users 1 to N, divides by N to get a percentile (0 to 1), multiplies by 5, and rounds up.
snapshot_counts[, K := ceiling(5 * (frank(journey_days, ties.method = "min") / .N))]

# 3. Expand the table: Duplicate rows so each user has K rows
cutoffs <- snapshot_counts[rep(1:.N, K)]

# 4. Generate random cutoffs and a unique snapshot_id
cutoffs[, random_cutoff := start_ts + runif(.N) * as.numeric(difftime(end_ts, start_ts, units="secs"))]

# Create a unique ID for each snapshot (e.g., "User123_1", "User123_2")
cutoffs[, snapshot_id := paste0(id, "_", rowid(id))]
```

### join K cutoffs back to train_events

```{r}
#| label: join K cutoffs back to train_events

# Duplicate the timestamp so it survives the join
train_events[, event_ts_dup := event_timestamp]

# Perform the Time Machine Join: Only keep events BEFORE the random cutoff
train_events_expanded <- train_events[cutoffs[, .(id, snapshot_id, random_cutoff)], 
                                      on = .(id, event_ts_dup <= random_cutoff), 
                                      nomatch = NULL, 
                                      allow.cartesian = TRUE]

# Cleanup column names post-join
setnames(train_events_expanded, "event_ts_dup", "cutoff_time") # The join condition column holds the cutoff timestamp

# Rename snapshot_id to 'id' so your function treats each snapshot as a unique user
setnames(train_events_expanded, "id", "original_user_id")
setnames(train_events_expanded, "snapshot_id", "id")


# Free up memory before flattening
rm(train_events, cutoffs, snapshot_counts); gc()


# Execute the flattening (This might take a few minutes depending on CPU)
train_flat <- flatten_journey(train_events_expanded)



# Rename 'id' back to 'snapshot_id' for clarity
setnames(train_flat, "id", "snapshot_id")

# extract the original ID out of the snapshot string
train_flat[, id := sub("_[0-9]+$", "", snapshot_id)]

# Add the final labels back to the flattened training data
train_flat <- merge(train_flat, 
                    user_outcomes[id %in% train_ids, .(id, final_outcome)], 
                    by = "id")

# Free up the expanded raw events
rm(train_events_expanded); gc()

# save k-cutoff sample
write_parquet(train_flat, "data/processed/train_features_kcut_sample.parquet")

rm(list=ls()); gc()
```
